metadata.summary: Build and maintain employer brand across Glassdoor, LinkedIn, careers pages, and testimonials with structured audit, content strategy, and human-approval gates. Requires a completed EVP.

content.title: Employer Branding
content.summary: Strategy and execution methodology for building and maintaining an employer brand across Glassdoor, LinkedIn, careers page, employee testimonials, and content calendars. Requires a completed EVP as input. The testable rule: every external-facing brand asset must trace back to a named EVP pillar, and no asset containing DEI claims, compensation data, or employee quotes may be published without human approval.

why.p: Employer brand drives application quality and quantity independent of job-board spend. Review monitoring within 48 hours improves rating trend; mechanical calendars without novelty injection decay in 4-6 weeks. AI-generated testimonials that read synthetic and Glassdoor responses that sound defensive are documented reputation risks. Human approval before any external publish is the non-negotiable gate.

list.item: Annual or quarterly employer-brand audit: scrape reviews, benchmark vs. competitors, identify perception-reality gaps.
Producing a 4-12 week content calendar across LinkedIn, Instagram, careers blog.
Drafting and rotating employee testimonials from employee-supplied raw material.
Monitoring and responding to Glassdoor/Indeed/Comparably reviews within 48h SLA.
Building and nurturing a talent community (rejected-but-strong candidates).
No EVP or culture document exists — do `employee-value-proposition` methodology first.
Crisis comms (layoffs, PR incident) — switch to crisis-communication workflow; brand agents produce tone-deaf copy during crises.
Highly regulated industries where every external post requires compliance review before agents can draft but not publish.
Sub-30-employee companies — the founder's voice is the brand; structured calendars are overkill.

section.name: brand-audit-framework
section.p: Perception-vs-reality audit process using Glassdoor, LinkedIn, candidate surveys, and exit interviews.

rule.statement: Run brand audits annually at minimum. A gap greater than 1.0 on a 5-point scale between internal perception (employee surveys) and external perception (Glassdoor/Comparably) signals repositioning is needed.
rule.rationale: A large perception gap means either the EVP over-promises (candidates arrive disappointed) or the brand under-communicates genuine strengths (missed talent).

rule.statement: Compare each EVP pillar against 3-5 direct talent competitors. Identify where you lead, where you are table-stakes, and where you trail — and never claim leadership where you trail.
rule.rationale: Candidates research employers on Glassdoor and LinkedIn before applying. Positioning claims that contradict visible competitor data undermine recruiter credibility in screening calls.

rule.statement: Respond to every Glassdoor review within 48 hours, including positive ones. Cap responses at 80 words. Never use "We are committed to..." Never dispute employee experience; only describe actions taken or planned.
rule.rationale: Over-defensive responses go viral and amplify negative reviews. Responses that dispute employee perception demonstrate the exact culture problems reviewers described.

list.item: Glassdoor: overall rating, review themes (pros/cons frequency), response rate trend.
LinkedIn: follower growth rate, organic post engagement, career page conversion rate.
Candidate surveys: post-interview NPS, "why declined" categories, source attribution.
Employee surveys: eNPS, top-ranked EVP pillars vs. bottom-ranked, open-text themes.
Exit interviews: top 3 stated reasons for leaving, manager-specific patterns.

antipattern.description: Auto-publishing Glassdoor responses without human approval.
antipattern.reason: Agent-generated responses that contradict employee experience or sound legalistic create PR incidents that are harder to recover from than the original review.

section.reference: Response structure for Glassdoor reviews: acknowledge, describe action, 80-word cap.
section.p: Tools and services for brand auditing include Glassdoor for Employers (reviews API for enterprise tiers), Indeed Employer and Comparably (limited APIs, mostly UI-driven), LinkedIn Talent Solutions (career pages and analytics via API), and reputation monitoring platforms like Reputation.com and ReviewTrackers.

section.name: content-strategy
section.p: Content calendar structure, channel-specific voice rules, and employee testimonial intake pipeline.

rule.statement: Rotate four content types in a 4-week repeating cycle: employee spotlight, behind-the-scenes, culture moment, job opening promo. Every slot must trace back to a named EVP pillar.
rule.rationale: Mechanical calendars without novelty injection decay after 4-6 weeks of engagement. The 4-type rotation balances brand-building with hiring intent and gives the algorithm enough variety to avoid suppression.

list.item: Week 1: Employee spotlight (LinkedIn) — personal story tied to Growth or Purpose pillar.
Week 2: Behind-the-scenes (Instagram/video) — ≤90s, culture moment in action.
Week 3: Culture moment (LinkedIn long-form) — ≤300 words, specific event or milestone.
Week 4: Open role promo (LinkedIn + Indeed) — paired with "what we're building" context post.

rule.statement: Never use the same post copy across channels. LinkedIn requires professional framing; Instagram requires visual-first brevity; careers blog allows long-form authenticity.
rule.rationale: Channel voice mismatch produces flat copy that is suppressed by algorithms and dismissed by audiences. The same text on LinkedIn and TikTok will underperform on both.

rule.statement: Cap LinkedIn hashtags at 3-5 per post. Never stuff more than 5.
rule.rationale: LinkedIn best practice is 3-5 hashtags; more signals low-quality content and suppresses organic reach.

rule.statement: Every employee testimonial must have a signed consent form and image release before publication. Use DRAFT-only status with [NAME] [TITLE] tokens until consent is captured and confirmed.
rule.rationale: Publishing testimonials without consent exposes employees and the company to legal liability. Agents must never auto-publish; always require explicit human confirmation that consent is on file.

rule.statement: Require employee-supplied raw quotes before drafting testimonials. Never synthesize quotes from job-offer letters, LinkedIn profiles, or performance reviews.
rule.rationale: AI-generated testimonials that were not said by real employees are ethically indefensible and legally risky if used in candidate-facing materials.

antipattern.description: Repurposing one testimonial format across all channels without adaptation.
antipattern.reason: A 300-word LinkedIn testimonial becomes unreadable on Instagram; a 30-second video quote loses context in a blog post. Repurpose with channel-specific editing, not copy-paste.

section.reference: 4-week brand content plan with per-slot hook, body, CTA, asset brief.
Employee testimonial intake: raw quote fields, consent checklist, approval flow.

section.name: rules-and-gates
section.p: Human-approval gates, ToS constraints, and antipatterns for employer branding agent workflows.

rule.statement: Any external publication — LinkedIn post, Glassdoor response, Instagram content, blog post — requires human approval before publish. Agents draft; humans approve and post.
rule.rationale: Brand voice drift, DEI misrepresentation, and legally risky compensation claims are impossible to fully prevent at generation time. The approval gate is the only reliable safeguard.

rule.statement: DEI claims (diversity percentages, ERG names, inclusion programs) must be sourced from HRIS data, not synthesized. Every DEI claim in a draft must have a data citation or be marked UNVERIFIED.
rule.rationale: Invented diversity statistics are a serious legal and reputational risk. Candidates and journalists verify claims; inaccurate statistics cause brand damage that exceeds the original benefit.

rule.statement: Do not scrape Glassdoor or Indeed without their official API (Glassdoor for Employers tier). Automated scraping without API access violates ToS and can result in employer account suspension.
rule.rationale: A suspended employer account on Glassdoor removes all company review responses and brand profile, causing permanent brand damage that exceeds the value of scraped data.

section.p: Use UTM-tagged careers page links per channel for source attribution; agents must add UTMs by default. Diversity content should lead with employee voices, never with leadership claims; metrics only when published quarterly internally. Pair every "we're hiring" post with a "what we're building" post — applications follow trust, not job listings. Repurpose content efficiently: one long-form testimonial → 5 LinkedIn quotes → 1 video → 1 newsletter feature.

antipattern.description: Cliché injection in every brand draft: "passionate team," "world-class culture," "amazing people."
antipattern.reason: These phrases appear on 90%+ of employer brand pages and provide zero differentiation. They train candidates to ignore the content.

antipattern.description: Generating fake employee names or composite quotes when raw material is missing.
antipattern.reason: Fabricated employee voices are unethical and constitute false advertising in candidate-facing materials. Always require real employee input before drafting.

antipattern.description: Brand voice drift across 50+ content generations without re-anchoring.
antipattern.reason: Agents drift from the established voice even when the brand guide is in context. Include 3 approved sample posts in every content generation prompt to re-anchor tone.

antipattern.description: Hashtag stuffing: LLMs add 15+ hashtags per post.
antipattern.reason: LinkedIn best practice is 3–5 per post; overstuffing signals low-quality content and suppresses reach.

antipattern.description: Image rights violations: agents pull stock images that may be unlicensed.
antipattern.reason: Use whitelisted libraries only (Unsplash, company DAM) to avoid legal exposure.

antipattern.description: Localization mishap: a US-positive phrase ("crushing it") translates poorly across markets.
antipattern.reason: Agents must localize per market, not direct-translate; market-specific language is the agent's responsibility before submission.

section.p: Best practices summary: audit perception (external) vs. reality (internal surveys) annually; gap > 1.0 on 5-pt scale = repositioning needed. Respond to every Glassdoor review within 48h, including positive ones; pattern-match, don't templatize. Get written employee consent + image release before testimonial publication; store in HRIS. Never auto-publish any external brand asset; always human approval on external content, DEI claims, compensation claims, and employee quotes.

template.purpose: 4-week brand content plan with channel, hook, body, CTA, asset brief per slot.
Response structure for negative/mixed reviews: acknowledge, describe action, cap 80 words.
Employee testimonial intake: raw quote fields, consent checklist, publication approval flow.
